weatherForecast.comment: The night into Saturday and the first half of the day will be mostly cloudy, but it will remain largely free of precipitation. The weather will improve somewhat during the daytime changes, especially south of the main Alpine ridge, with a few sunny spells and the peaks becoming partly clear. Temperatures will be around -1 degrees at 2000 metres and between 0 and +3 degrees at 1500 metres. Only moderate winds with gusts of up to 40 km/h from south to north-west.

avalancheActivity.highlights: 15 to 30 cm of new fallen snow with wind on a weak old snowpack! Tricky situation for winter sports in open terrain!
avalancheActivity.comment: 15 to 30 cm of new fallen snow was deposited on a soft snow surface or surface hoar under moderate to strong winds. Medium-sized slab avalanches can be triggered in some places. Naturally triggered avalanches and remote triggering are also possible. Fractures can tear through the poorly built-up persistent weak layer. The avalanche prone locations are barely recognisable and should be assessed defensively. Take particular care in the transition from a little to a lot of snow and in the blown-in entrance areas of gullies and bowls.

snowpackStructure.comment: The new fallen snow was deposited on a poorly built-up old snowpack in mostly moderate winds. Due to the weather conditions, a bound layer (board) formed. On the one hand, fractures occur within the old snowpack, which often consists of a sequence of melt-freeze crusts and angular forms or deep rime. On the other hand, existing weak layers can be disturbed at the transition from new fallen snow to old snowpack. Below around 1600 metres, the snowpack is moist.

tendency.highlights:
On Friday, there will be a brief intermittent high with some sunny spells. In the afternoon, it will start to get cloudy again from the west.
The avalanche danger decreases slightly.

weatherForecast.comment: It will snow heavily on the northern side of the Alps during the night to Saturday, with snowfall levels between 1000 and 1200 metres. Saturday will start mostly cloudy, with a few snow showers possible in the north. The weather will improve tentatively during the daytime changes, but there is little chance of longer sunny spells and the peaks will only rarely be clear. Temperatures at 2000 metres will be around -2 degrees, at 1500 metres between 0 and +2 degrees. The wind will be moderate to brisk with gusts of up to 40 km/h from the south to north-west.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Persistent weak layer problem in northern and eastern sectors, covered by fresh wind slab! Wind slabs and persistent weak layers are still prone to triggering
avalancheActivity.comment: The avalanche risk is assessed as moderate above the tree line and low below. There is still a persistent weak layer problem in the northern and eastern exposures. In addition, fresh pillows of wind drifted snow can be triggered as small slab avalanches by low additional loads. Medium-sized slab avalanches are also possible when breaking through the weak old snowpack. The danger areas are mainly located in the transition from little to more snow as well as behind terrain edges and in the entrance area of gullies and bowls. The avalanche prone locations are often difficult to recognise.

snowpackStructure.comment: Depending on the amount of new snow and the effect of the wind, new and wind slab snow was deposited on a soft snow surface in places and on surface hoar in sheltered locations. The wind slab in particular is still prone to triggering in higher, steeper areas. There is significantly more snow in shady gullies and bowls than on sunny slopes. In the northern and eastern exposures, the old snowpack continues to be weakened by crusts and layers with faceted crystals and floating snow; the persistent weak layer problem remains. There is still significantly less snow than average. Below around 1600 metres, the snowpack is wet.

tendency.highlights:
On Friday, there will be a brief intermittent high with some sunny spells. In the afternoon, it will start to get cloudy again from the west.
The avalanche danger decreases slightly.

weatherForecast.comment: The night into Saturday and the first half of the day will be mostly cloudy, but it will remain largely free of precipitation. The weather will improve somewhat during the daytime changes, especially south of the main Alpine ridge, with a few sunny spells and the peaks becoming partly clear. Temperatures at 2000 metres will be between -1 and +2 degrees, at 1500 metres between +1 and +4 degrees. Moderate winds from south to north-west.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Low avalanche danger with mostly little snow.
avalancheActivity.comment: The avalanche risk is low. In a few places, especially in locations above the tree line, snowdrift accumulations can be triggered as small slab avalanches in steep gullies and bowls. Avalanche prone locations and icy areas can be covered with a thin layer of fresh snow. Small, wet slides cannot be ruled out at altitudes below 1500 metres.

snowpackStructure.comment: There is still significantly less snow than average. A thin, partly bound snowpack covers a very variable and mostly hard old snowpack. In shady gullies and bowls, the connection between the bound snowpack and the old snowpack is sometimes poor. In addition, angular shapes can weaken the snow base, fundament.

tendency.highlights:
On Sunday, the sun will often shine over the peaks, but in the morning it will still often be cloudy and foggy below 2000 metres.
No change in the avalanche danger is expected.

weatherForecast.comment: The last rain and snowfall will subside on Thursday evening and the cloud cover will soften, especially in the northern Alps. After the dissolution of residual clouds and fog, Friday will temporarily bring quite sunny mountain weather, but clouds will return from the south-west in high and medium-high layers during the afternoon. However, it will remain dry until the evening. The wind will blow moderately to strongly from a south-westerly to north-westerly direction. Temperatures at 2000 metres will be between -2 and +2 degrees, at 1500 metres between -1 and +4 degrees.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Low avalanche danger, but persistent weak layer problems in the northern and eastern exposures!
avalancheActivity.comment: The avalanche risk is low. In the northern and eastern exposures, there is still a persistent weak layer problem at altitudes above the tree line. In addition, the strong to stormy southerly winds have created fresh pillows of wind drifted snow. In steep terrain, these can be triggered in a few places. However, the avalanches remain small even if they tear through to the old snowpack. In general, the risk of entrainment, falling and injury outweighs the risk of burial. However, be careful in the area of terrain traps.

snowpackStructure.comment: There is still significantly less snow than average. A thin, partly bound snowpack covers a very variable and mostly hard old snowpack. There is significantly more snow in shady gullies and bowls than on sunny slopes, some of which are partly bare up to 2000 metres. In the northern and eastern exposures, the snowpack is still weakened by layers with faceted crystals and floating snow (persistent weak layer problem!).

tendency.highlights:
On Sunday, the sun will often shine over the peaks, but in the morning it will still often be cloudy and foggy below 2000 metres.
The persistent weak layer problem remains.

ALBINA.generalHeadline: Note the persistent weak layer problem in the northern and eastern exposures!
